game:seafarer-quickstart-text-logbarge:
Log Barge
From the log of Captain Silas Fenrick, 'the Rust Rider,' aboard the Three Headed Monkey: Every great voyage begins with a terrible idea and a pile of logs. The log barge won't win any races — she's wide, flat, and stubborn in a crosswind — but she'll carry more cargo than any canoe ever dreamed of, and she'll do it from the stone age onward. Building her Lash together 12 Logs, 18 Sticks, and 6 Ropes in the crafting grid. The wood you choose sets the look of the finished deck — I favor oak myself, but a redwood barge does turn heads in port. Getting moving She needs muscle to move. Grab an Oar, take the back seat, and start paddling. A second soul can ride up front and enjoy the view — or keep watch for rocks. For a bit more speed, rig up a Raft sail from Sticks, Linen, and Rope. Attach it to the sail mount with ctrl + and let the wind earn its keep. Loading up Six expansion slots line the port and starboard sides — fore, mid, and aft on each. Strap on Chests, Baskets, Storage Vessels, Barrels, Lanterns, or Oil Lamps with ctrl + . The fore slots also take rope tie posts — handy for when you don't trust the current. Access any container's inventory with once it's placed. Stowing the barge Need to portage? Strip off all storage and accessories first, then Shift + with an empty backpack slot to hoist the whole thing. Right click to set her back down on water or land.
seafarer:itemdesc-boat-logbarge: A primitive barge designed for long coastal voyages. Its wide deck supports storage and animals for moving supplies along the shore.
seafarer:itemdesc-raftsail: A simple linen sail for catching coastal winds. Attach to a log barge to increase speed.
seafarer:itemdesc-chili: A fiery pepper. Roast on a griddle, dry on a frame, or toss into a meal for some heat.
seafarer:itemdesc-corn: A staple grain. Dry it, then soak with limewater in a barrel to unlock its full nutrition.
seafarer:itemdesc-potato: A starchy tuber. Roast it on a fire for a hearty meal, or toss it into a soup or stew.
seafarer:itemdesc-sugarcane: Sweet cane stalk. Press on a prep table for juice, then boil into molasses or sugar.
seafarer:itemdesc-tomato: A juicy fruit. Sear on a griddle, dry on a frame, or use in soups, stews, and pies.
seafarer:itemdesc-driedtomato: Sun-dried tomato with concentrated flavor. Keeps for a very long time.
seafarer:blockdesc-dryingframe: For air-drying food outdoors. Wind speeds drying, but rain causes spoilage.
seafarer:dryingframe-not-exposed: Not exposed to sky — drying won't work!
seafarer:itemdesc-saltrubbedmeat: Freshly rubbed with salt on a prep table. Leave it to cure into salted meat.
item-driedsaltedmeat-premiumfish-nuggetp: Dried salted premium fish nugget (part baked)
seafarer:itemdesc-saltedmeat: Cured and salted. Place on a drying frame to dry it for long-term storage.
seafarer:itemdesc-driedsaltedmeat: Salt-cured and air-dried. Keeps without spoiling. Soak in a barrel of water to rehydrate for cooking.
seafarer:itemdesc-slackedmeat: Rehydrated salted meat. Ready for pies, stews, soups, or searing on a griddle.
seafarer:blockdesc-saltpan: A shallow clay pan for evaporating saltwater into salt. Place outdoors with clear sky above.
seafarer:saltpan-no-sky: Not exposed to sky — evaporation paused
seafarer:itemdesc-crushedshell: Broken shell fragments. Stack on the ground and fire in a pit kiln to produce lime.
seafarer:itemdesc-liveclam: A live clam dug from muddy gravel. Place on the ground and sneak + right-click with a knife to shuck for meat and shell.
seafarer:itemdesc-mudrake: A raking tool for sifting through muddy gravel to find clams and other shellfish. Hold right-click on muddy gravel to rake.
seafarer:itemdesc-mudrake-head: A smithed rake head. Combine with a stick to make a mud rake.
seafarer:blockdesc-waterskin: A primitive leather water container made from cured pelts. Holds 4 litres of liquid. Available from the stone age — no pottery needed.
seafarer:blockdesc-amphora: A tall clay vessel designed for transporting liquids. Holds 10 litres of any liquid. Seal with beeswax or fat to preserve contents for long voyages.
seafarer:blockdesc-amphora-storage: A wide-bodied clay vessel for storing dry goods and materials. Holds 8 inventory slots. Seal with beeswax or fat to preserve contents.
seafarer:blockdesc-amphora-raw: An unfired amphora. Fire in a kiln to harden.
seafarer:blockdesc-amphora-storage-raw: An unfired storage amphora. Fire in a kiln to harden.
seafarer:amphora-is-sealed: This amphora is sealed. Hold and sneak+right-click to unseal.
seafarer:amphora-storage-is-sealed: This amphora is sealed. Sneak+right-click to unseal.
seafarer:amphora-empty-or-sealed: This amphora is empty or already sealed.
game:seafarer-quickstart-text-saltpan:
Salt Pan
From the log of Captain Silas Fenrick, 'the Rust Rider,' aboard the Three Headed Monkey: Salt is the sailor's best friend — it keeps meat from going foul, it trades well at any port, and in a pinch you can rub it on a wound and pretend you're brave. The salt pan is how you make your own, and the beauty of it is you can start before you've even built a proper kiln. Making one Shape a salt pan on the Clayforming table, then fire it in a Pit Kiln. Simple as that. Harvesting salt Set the fired pan outdoors under open sky. Fill it with saltwater from a Bucket or any liquid container — it holds 10 litres and you can top it off gradually. Then you wait. The sun does the work. Hotter climates dry faster, but rain will stall the process, and a roof blocks it entirely. When the water's gone and white crystals line the pan, hold right-click to scrape out your Salt. A Shovel speeds this up considerably — iron or steel and you'll have it in half a heartbeat. A Knife works too, though not as fast. Bare hands will get it done eventually. Captain's advice Line up a dozen pans along a sunny beach and you've got yourself a proper salt farm. I've seen a man retire on salt alone — though I suspect he was terribly bored.
game:seafarer-quickstart-text-preservati:
Salting, Drying & Soaking
From the log of Captain Silas Fenrick, 'the Rust Rider,' aboard the Three Headed Monkey: A ship is only as good as its provisions, and fresh meat turns mutinous after two days at sea. The trick the old salts taught me — and I'll pass it to you free of charge — is a three-step process: rub it, dry it, soak it when you're ready to eat. Step 1 — Salt rubbing Set up a Prep Table with an empty Barrel on the platform slot, then fill the barrel with Salt. Place a Salt Pan in the tool slot. Now right-click raw meat onto the work surface — it'll come back as Salt-rubbed meat, one salt consumed per piece. The salt pulls moisture from the surface and starts the cure. Give it time and it'll become proper salted meat on its own. No prep table handy? I've done it straight on a Salt Pan full of salt in a pinch — just press the meat right onto it. Desperate times, fellow voyager. Step 2 — Drying Hang your Salted meat on a Drying Frame under open sky and let the wind do its work. After a few days you'll have Dried salted meat — hard as ship planking and twice as long-lasting. Mind the rain, though. Wet meat on a frame goes bad fast. The frame's good for more than meat, too. Tomatoes, Chilies, Coconut meat, and Corn all take well to drying. Step 3 — Soaking (slacking) Now, dried salted meat will last you to the edge of the world and back, but you wouldn't want to eat it as-is unless you enjoy chewing on a salt lick. Seal it in a Barrel with fresh water, and after a good soak it becomes Slacked meat — tender, mild, and ready for the pot. Slacked meat makes fine Stews, Soups, and Pies. Sear it on a Griddle and it's good enough for a captain's table. The short of it Raw meat → Salt-rubbed (prep table or salt pan) → Salted (patience) → Dried salted (drying frame) → Slacked (barrel + water) → Ready to cook.
game:seafarer-quickstart-text-corn:
Corn
From the log of Captain Silas Fenrick, 'the Rust Rider,' aboard the Three Headed Monkey: I'll confess I didn't think much of corn when I first saw it growing on some sun-blasted island. Looked plain. Tasted plain. But then a woman at port showed me the trick the locals had known for ages — soak it in limewater first, and the whole grain opens up. More nutrition, better flavor, grinds like a dream. They call it nixtamalization. I call it the reason my crew stopped complaining about the food. Growing it Plant Corn seeds on Farmland like any crop. It grows through several stages — be patient. When it's tall and ready, break the stalks to collect your corn. Drying Fresh corn's fine to eat, but it won't keep. Hang it on a Drying Frame to get Dried corn. Lasts much longer, and you'll need it dried for the next step anyway. Nixtamalization Seal your Dried corn with limewater in a Barrel. The alkaline soak breaks down the tough hull and unlocks nutrients you'd otherwise miss entirely. What comes out is Nixtamal — soft, fragrant, and ready for the quern. Grinding into masa Run Nixtamal through a Quern to produce Masa — a fine, smooth corn dough. The foundation of everything good that follows. Tortillas and beyond Mix Masa with water in the crafting grid for Tortilla dough, then slap it on a Griddle. Fresh Tortillas — warm, simple, and deeply satisfying after weeks of hardtack. Wrap one around some fillings and you've got a Burrito. And for the thirsty Corn ferments nicely in a barrel into Corn beer. Distill that and you get Corn whiskey — rough stuff, but it warms the bones on a cold night watch. The short of it Corn seeds → Grow → Harvest → Dry on frame → Soak with limewater (barrel) → Nixtamal → Grind (quern) → Masa → Mix with water → Tortilla dough → Cook on griddle → Tortillas.
game:seafarer-quickstart-text-coconut:
Coconut
From the log of Captain Silas Fenrick, 'the Rust Rider,' aboard the Three Headed Monkey: The coconut palm is the most generous tree I've ever met. Water, meat, milk, oil, and if you're patient enough, a passable drink that'll make you forget you're stranded on an island. I've provisioned entire voyages off a good stand of palms. Growing them Plant a Coconut tree cutting and wait. They only thrive in warm, tropical climates, and they take their sweet time growing — but once they're mature, they'll keep producing. Harvesting Mature palms bear Coconuts. Grab them when they're ripe. Cracking open Set a coconut on a Prep Table to crack it in two and get an Open coconut. No prep table? Drop the coconut on the ground and give it a whack with a Knife — works just as well, if a bit less civilized. The water Pour the Coconut water into a container first. It's light, refreshing, and the best thing you'll drink on a hot day that isn't rum. The meat Use a knife on the open half to pry out the Coconut meat — works on a prep table or right there on the ground. Once you've got the flesh, you have options: - Eat it raw — nothing wrong with that - Toast it on a Griddle for Toasted coconut — crispy, nutty, dangerously snackable - Dry it on a Drying Frame for Dried coconut that'll keep for ages - Press it on the prep table for Coconut milk From milk to oil Seal Coconut milk in a Barrel and let time do its work. The fat separates into Coconut oil — a long-lasting cooking fat that my quartermaster swears by. And for the evenings Ferment coconut water in a barrel for Coconut toddy. Distill that into Coconut arrack, and you've got something that'll put hair on a barnacle. The short of it Coconut tree → Harvest → Crack open (prep table or knife) → Pour out water → Extract meat (knife) → Toast, dry, eat raw, or press for milk → Seal milk in barrel → Coconut oil.
game:seafarer-quickstart-text-shellfish:
Shellfish & Lime
From the log of Captain Silas Fenrick, 'the Rust Rider,' aboard the Three Headed Monkey: Muddy gravel along riverbeds and shorelines is full of treasure if you know how to look. Clams, scallops, the odd gold nugget — and enough shells to keep a mason in lime for a season. Raking for shellfish Craft a Mud Rake — a wooden one needs just sticks, or smith a metal head for better drop rates. Use it to break Muddy gravel and the rake will turn up shells, Live clams, and if you're lucky, a bit of Gold. Better rakes mean better odds. Shucking clams Set a Live clam on the ground, then Shift + with a Knife to shuck it. You'll get a piece of Premium fish meat and a Clam shell. A Prep Table with a knife in the tool slot does the same job, faster. Crushing shells Any Seashell can be crushed into Crushed shell fragments. Set the shell on the ground and Shift + with a Hammer or Stone. The Prep Table works too — put a hammer in the tool slot and feed it shells. Burning shells for lime Stack your Crushed shells on the ground — they pile up like bricks, up to 32 in a stack. Cover them with fuel and fire them in a Pit kiln. Each shell burns into one Lime. Whole shells work in the kiln too, but they won't stack together if they're different types. Crushing first lets you combine clams, scallops, and conches into one efficient pile. If you've got a Quern, grinding shells gives two lime per shell — slower but more efficient. The short of it Muddy gravel + Mud rake → Shells & live clams → Shuck clams (knife) → Crush shells (hammer/stone) → Stack & fire in pit kiln → Lime.
game:seafarer-quickstart-text-griddle:
Griddle & Griddle Hearth
From the log of Captain Silas Fenrick, 'the Rust Rider,' aboard the Three Headed Monkey: I used to think cooking meant throwing things into a pot and hoping for the best. Then my ship's cook — a terrifying woman named Birta — showed me the griddle, and I've never looked back. A flat surface, a hot fire, and whatever you've got on hand. Seared fish, fresh tortillas, roasted chilies that'll wake the dead. Every camp and galley deserves one. Making a griddle Start simple — shape a Clay griddle on the Clayforming table and fire it in a Pit Kiln. Ceramic works fine, but if you've got metal to spare, cast a proper one from copper, bronze, iron, or steel using a Griddle mold. Setting up the hearth Craft a Griddle Hearth from stone in the crafting grid. Place it down, then set your griddle on top with . That's your cooking station. Fire it up Feed the hearth firewood with and light it with a torch or firestarter. She'll hold temperature as long as there's fuel to burn. Cooking Drop ingredients onto the hot surface with and let them sizzle. Pull them off the same way when they're done. Simple, fast, and the smell alone will have your crew gathering 'round. What you can cook - Flatbread — any Flour dough, quick and filling - Oiled flatbread — richer, cooked with Oil or fat - Tortilla — fresh from Tortilla dough, worth every step of the corn process - Seared meat — bushmeat, red meat, or poultry, hot and fast - Seared slacked meat — for when you've rehydrated your provisions - Seared fish and Seared premium fish — the catch of the day - Pan-seared fish and Pan-seared premium fish — with a splash of oil, even better - Roasted chili — charred until blistered, a real crowd-splitter - Toasted coconut — golden, nutty, and gone before it cools - Seared tomato — charred and sweet - Burrito — stuff a tortilla, fold it up, sear it shut. Birta's favorite.
game:seafarer-quickstart-text-sugar:
Sugar Production
From the log of Captain Silas Fenrick, 'the Rust Rider,' aboard the Three Headed Monkey: Sugar is currency in the tropics — sometimes more reliable than coin. It sweetens food, preserves fruit, and when you're feeling enterprising, you can turn the stuff that makes it into rum. I've run sugar between ports more times than I can count, and it always sells. Growing sugar cane Plant Sugar cane seeds on Farmland. It favors warm climates and grows tall before it's ready. Harvest the mature stalks to collect Sugar cane. Pressing into juice Take your cane to a Prep Table and press it. You'll get Cane juice — sweet, perishable, and the start of everything. You'll also get Cane mush as a byproduct — don't throw it away. Dry it on a Drying Frame and it becomes Bagasse, which burns like brown coal. Free fuel. Boiling into molasses Pour Cane juice into a Cooking pot and cook it down. What you get is Molasses — thick, dark, and deeply sweet. Good for cooking on its own, and the base for the next step. Cooking into sugar Keep cooking. Put Molasses back in the cooking pot and boil it further. The water drives off and you're left with crystallized Sugar — 4 pieces per litre of molasses. A valuable sweetener, preservative, and trade good. Sugar syrup Dissolve Sugar in water in a Barrel to make Sugar syrup. On its own it's a fine sweetener for cooking. But the real trick is this — seal fruit in a barrel with sugar syrup and you get Candied fruit. Keeps practically forever and tastes like shore leave. Rum If you've got a Condenser and a Boiler, you can distill your way to spirits. Cane juice distills into White rum — clean and sharp. Molasses distills into Dark rum — richer, warmer, and the crew's favorite by a wide margin. The short of it Sugar cane → Press (prep table) → Cane juice → Cook (pot) → Molasses → Cook again → Sugar → Dissolve in water (barrel) → Sugar syrup → Seal with fruit (barrel) → Candied fruit. Alternatively: Cane juice → Distill → White rum. Molasses → Distill → Dark rum.
game:seafarer-quickstart-text-burrito:
Burrito
From the log of Captain Silas Fenrick, 'the Rust Rider,' aboard the Three Headed Monkey: Birta invented this one — or so she claims. Take a fresh tortilla, wrap it around whatever you've got, and sear it shut on the griddle. Feeds a hungry sailor faster than anything else I've seen, and you can stuff just about anything in there. Step 1 — Make a tortilla You'll need a Tortilla first. See the Corn guide for the full process — corn to masa to dough to griddle. Step 2 — Place the tortilla With the tortilla in hand, on any flat surface to lay it down as a raw burrito. It'll sit there open, waiting for fillings. Step 3 — Add fillings Hold a filling ingredient and on the placed burrito. You can add up to two fillings. Valid fillings include: - Vegetables — any variety, fresh or preserved - Raw meat — red meat, bushmeat, poultry, or fish - Slacked meat — whole or cut - Cheese — sliced cheddar or blue - Eggs - Tomatoes, chilies (fresh, dried, or roasted), corn, coconut meat - Candied fruit — for those with a sweet tooth Step 4 — Cook it Grab the filled raw burrito with an empty hand, then place it on a hot Griddle with . It'll sear shut and come out golden and ready to eat. The short of it Tortilla → Place on surface → Add up to 2 fillings → Grab → Cook on griddle → Burrito.
seafarer:blockdesc-onggi: A large clay jar that works like a 20-litre barrel. Use it for any barrel recipe — fermentation, brining, soaking, and more.
seafarer:blockdesc-onggi-raw: An unfired onggi jar. Fire in a kiln to harden.
seafarer:itemdesc-driedchili: Dried chili pepper. Ferment in a barrel for hot sauce or kimchi.
seafarer:itemdesc-shreddedcabbage: Finely shredded cabbage, ready for fermentation into kimchi.
seafarer:itemdesc-kimchi: Spicy fermented cabbage. Long-lasting and nutritious. Good in stews, soups, and pies.
seafarer:itemdesc-fermentedvegetable: Brined and fermented. Keeps for a long time. Good in stews, soups, and pies.
seafarer:itemdesc-fermentedmeat: Salted meat fermented in brine. Extremely long-lasting. Good in stews and soups.
seafarer:itemdesc-garumportion: A pungent fermented fish sauce, rich in protein.
seafarer:itemdesc-soysauceportion: A dark, savory fermented sauce. Adds depth to any meal.
seafarer:itemdesc-hotsauceportion: Fermented chili sauce. Adds heat to any meal.
seafarer:itemdesc-misoportion: A thick, savory fermented paste. Rich in protein and umami. Good in soups and stews.
seafarer:tortilladough-note-text: Grow corn, dry it, soak dried corn with limewater in a barrel to make nixtamal, grind in a quern to make masa, then mix masa with water.
seafarer:itemdesc-premiumfish: A premium cut of fish, rich in nutrients. Sear on a griddle or use in cooking.
seafarer:itemdesc-driedcorn: Dried corn kernels. Soak with limewater in a barrel to make nixtamal, or brew into corn beer.
seafarer:itemdesc-nixtamal: Corn treated with limewater. More nutritious and easier to grind. Process in a quern to make masa.
seafarer:itemdesc-masa: Ground nixtamal dough. Mix with water to make tortilla dough.
seafarer:itemdesc-coconut: A whole coconut. Crack open on a prep table to get at the water and meat inside.
seafarer:blockdesc-opencoconut: A cracked-open coconut. Pour out the water, then use a knife to extract the meat.
seafarer:itemdesc-coconutwaterportion: A light, refreshing drink. Can be brewed into coconut toddy.
seafarer:itemdesc-coconutmeat: Fresh coconut flesh. Eat raw, toast on a griddle, dry on a frame, or press for milk.
seafarer:itemdesc-coconutmilkportion: Rich, creamy coconut milk. Seal in a barrel to separate into oil, or use in cooking.
seafarer:itemdesc-toastedcoconut: Crispy toasted coconut with a rich, nutty flavor.
seafarer:itemdesc-coconutpressedmash: Dry pulp left over from pressing. Spoils quickly.
seafarer:itemdesc-driedcoconut: Dried coconut meat. Long-lasting and nutritious.
seafarer:itemdesc-canejuiceportion: Fresh juice pressed from sugar cane. Boil into molasses, or distill into white rum.
seafarer:itemdesc-molassesportion: Thick, dark syrup from boiled cane juice. Cook further to produce sugar, or distill into dark rum.
seafarer:itemdesc-sugar: Crystallized sugar. A valuable sweetener, preservative, and trade good.
seafarer:itemdesc-canemush: Wet pulp left over from pressing. Dry on a frame to make bagasse.
seafarer:itemdesc-bagasse: Dried sugar cane fiber. Burns like brown coal.
seafarer:itemdesc-spiritportion-whiterum: A clear spirit distilled from cane juice.
seafarer:itemdesc-spiritportion-darkrum: A rich spirit distilled from molasses.
seafarer:itemdesc-syrupportion: Sugar dissolved in water. Use as a sweetener, or seal fruit in it to make preserves.
seafarer:itemdesc-candiedfruit-*: Fruit preserved in sugar syrup. Long-lasting and sweet.
seafarer:burrito-empty: Add fillings by right-clicking with ingredients
seafarer:burrito-invalid-filling: This item cannot be used as a burrito filling
dialogue-morgan-welcome: Well now, a fresh face on the docks! Welcome to Tortuga, voyager. If you've got spirits to sell or need supplies for the voyage ahead, I'm your woman.
dialogue-morgan-welcomeback: Back again? Good to see you. What can I do for you today?
dialogue-morgan-name: Name's Morgan. Any problems or needs come see me. I keep things organized.
dialogue-morgan-profession: I guess I'm the unofficial town mayor now. Since the governor got eaten by a temporal rift. Doesn't require much work at the moment other than looking after the docks and warehouse.
dialogue-morgan-tortuga-lore: Tortuga's been a port for traders, seafarers, and pirates for years or at least it was. A temporal typhoon hit the port tearing through the buildings and ripping us out of the old world and into this rust world. We just woke up here, I'm sure your story is much the same.
dialogue-morgan-goodbye: Fair winds to you. Come back when you need restocking.
dialogue-morgan-ask-rebuilding: Can I help with the rebuilding effort?
dialogue-morgan-rebuilding-intro: I love the spirit, voyager. Have a talk with the other residents they'll have some work for you too. There was an old miner around here, went a little mad after we ran out of potatoes. If you can convince him to start mining again we'll be able to start smelting ingots.
dialogue-morgan-accept-mine: I'll track down the miner for you.
dialogue-morgan-mine-start: That's the spirit! Take this letter show it to the old Potato King. He lives under the mountain outside of town. Stubborn as bedrock but maybe he'll listen if he knows it's from me.
dialogue-morgan-have-contract: I have a signed contract from the Potato King.
dialogue-morgan-lost-letter: I lost the letter you gave me.
dialogue-morgan-remind-quest: What was I supposed to do again?
dialogue-morgan-mine-reminder: Find the Potato King and show him my letter and convince him to start mining again. We need him if we're ever going to produce ingots.
dialogue-morgan-replace-letter: Losing things at sea is nothing new. Here, take another copy. Don't lose this one.
dialogue-morgan-mine-contract-received: A signed contract! There are a lot of diagrams about conveyor belts and automation plans in here. But he seems willing to work again. You've made a real difference, voyager.
dialogue-morgan-mine-complete: I'm surprised, didn't really think you'd get this done. The first shipment of ore has already arrived. We can start producing copper ingots, nails, and plates again.
dialogue-morgan-rebuilding-done: You've already done so much for us, voyager. The mine is running again thanks to you. Talk to the others, they could use your help too.
dialogue-morgan-tradegoods-intro: Now that the mine is running, we need to build up our trade goods. Salt and sugar are worth their weight in gold out here. Bring me as much as you can find.
dialogue-morgan-accept-tradegoods: I'll bring you salt and sugar.
dialogue-morgan-tradegoods-start: That's the spirit. Bring me salt and sugar, as much as you can find. With enough of both we can actually start trading properly.
dialogue-morgan-tradegoods-status: How much salt and sugar do you still need?
dialogue-morgan-salt-status: That's {global.quest-seafarer-morgan-tradegoods-salt-progress} of 64 salt. I need more before I can start trading it.
dialogue-morgan-salt-complete: That's all the salt I need! I can finally offer something worth trading.
dialogue-morgan-sugar-thanks: Sugar. Sweet. We can do a lot with this.
dialogue-morgan-sugar-status: That's {global.quest-seafarer-morgan-tradegoods-sugar-progress} of 64 sugar. Keep it coming.
dialogue-morgan-sugar-complete: Enough sugar at last. I can find plenty of uses for it.
dialogue-morgan-tradegoods-statusreport: I need {global.quest-seafarer-morgan-tradegoods-salt-progress}/64 salt and {global.quest-seafarer-morgan-tradegoods-sugar-progress}/64 sugar. Bring what you can.
dialogue-morgan-tradegoods-complete: That's everything we needed. The trade goods are flowing and Tortuga is looking more like a real port every day. You've made a real difference, voyager.
quest-morgan-tradegoods-desc: Morgan needs salt and sugar to build up Tortuga's trade goods. Bring her 64 of each.
itemdesc-letter-morgan: A letter from Morgan, the provisioner of Tortuga, requesting the Potato King to resume mining operations.
itemdesc-signed-contract: A signed contract from the Potato King agreeing to resume mining operations for Tortuga.
itemdesc-map-crimsonrose: A weathered map showing the location of the Crimson Rose wreck.
itemdesc-map-potato: A hastily drawn map to an abandoned farm where the last potato seed can be found.
itemdesc-map-potatoking: A sketch from Morgan showing the way to the Potato King's mountain hideout.
itemdesc-map-tortuga: A weathered scroll tucked inside a sea-tumbled bottle, its ink speaking of a far-off port called Tortuga.
itemdesc-sealed-chest: A heavy sealed chest recovered from the wreck of the Crimson Rose.
itemdesc-varnishportion-marine: A thick waterproof varnish made from resin and fat. Apply to wood or ship hulls to make them watertight.
itemdesc-canvas-sail: A basic sail sewn from plain linen and rope. Not water resistant.
itemdesc-oiled-canvas: Canvas treated with oil to make it waterproof. Used for sails and weather protection.
itemdesc-oiled-canvas-sail: A sail sewn from oiled canvas and rope. Waterproof and durable.
itemdesc-waxed-canvas: Oiled canvas rubbed with wax for extra strength and water resistance.
itemdesc-waxed-canvas-sail: A sail made from waxed canvas. Stronger and more durable than oiled canvas.
itemdesc-plank-seasoned: Air-dried lumber that is lighter and stronger than fresh-cut wood. Essential for quality shipbuilding.
itemdesc-plank-varnished: A board treated with marine varnish. Waterproof and resistant to rot.
itemdesc-trainingbook-shipwright: A comprehensive guide to the fundamentals of shipbuilding. Reading this grants shipwright training.
itemdesc-trainingbook-bearhunter: A well-worn guide to tracking, trapping, and felling the great bears. Reading this grants Bear Hunter training.
itemdesc-trainingbook-buccaneer: A salt-stained journal filled with cutlass techniques, boarding tactics, and hard-won combat wisdom. Reading this grants Buccaneer training.
itemdesc-trainingbook-gardener: A weathered handbook of planting seasons, grafting technique, and orchard care. Reading this grants Gardener training.
blockdesc-grow-pot-*-fired: A shallow ceramic pot pre-filled with rich soil. Right-click with a fruit tree cutting to plant a dwarf variety — smaller, fruits much faster, lower overall yield.
blockdesc-grow-pot-*-raw: Unfired clay grow pot. Fire in a kiln to harden it.
blockcraftdesc-grow-pot-*-raw: Shallow pot for growing dwarf fruit trees
itemdesc-schematic-outrigger: Detailed plans for building an outrigger, a fast lightweight fishing boat.
itemdesc-cutlass-*: A short, sturdy sailor's blade. Built for the close quarters of a ship's deck.
itemdesc-arrow-barbed-*: An arrow with a wickedly barbed head. Causes the target to bleed for several seconds after impact, but is harder to pull out intact.
itemdesc-arrowhead-barbed-*: A wickedly barbed arrowhead. Twice the workshop time of a plain head. Ready to haft onto a shaft.
itemdesc-bladehead-cutlass-*: An unhafted cutlass blade. Combine with a stick to assemble the finished weapon.
seafarer:trainingbook-nosource: This book has no registered training.
quest-drake-tradeship-desc: Drake needs birch boards, rope, and linen to build a new sailboat that will let Tortuga trade with the outside world again.
quest-drake-tricks-desc: Learn marine varnish, oiled canvas, and sail-making from Drake to earn your Apprentice Shipwright's training.
quest-drake-seasoned-desc: Deliver 160 seasoned boards so Drake can work up plans for a proper outrigger.
quest-morgan-mine-desc: Convince the Potato King to start mining again. Deliver Morgan's letter and bring back a signed contract.
quest-celeste-crimsonrose-desc: Celeste's old ship went down with a sealed chest of her loot. Find the wreck site using the map she gave you and dig up the chest.
quest-celeste-rusthunter-desc: Thin the herd of rust monsters (drifters) around the port. Celeste wants ten dead.
quest-celeste-bearhunter-desc: Prove your hunting skills to Celeste by bringing her three different kinds of bear pelt — head and all.
dialogue-celeste-welcome: Another soul washing up on the shoals. I'd offer you a drink but the keg ran dry months ago.
dialogue-celeste-blackbronze-unlock: Now that the port's on its feet, I can part with a few finer blades and tools. Take a look next time you're trading.
dialogue-celeste-name: Celeste. Former captain of the Crimson Rose. Former buccaneer, adventurer and explorer.
dialogue-celeste-profession: These days I just try and keep the port safe from rust monsters and try and figure out how to make booze from salt water
dialogue-celeste-maps: Do you have any special maps?
dialogue-celeste-maps-info: I have a few charts and maps around. Not doing me any good anymore. You want one, I'll trade you from a decent drink.
dialogue-celeste-goodbye: Ya, we'll see if you come back.
dialogue-celeste-ask-crimsonrose: I heard you lost a ship, the Crimson Rose?
dialogue-celeste-crimsonrose-intro: Why do you lot keep bothering me with this nonsense. Sure, you want to help and I want my loot from the Crimson Rose.
dialogue-celeste-crimsonrose-start: Fine. Here's a map to where she went down. The chest should still be sealed, it was built to survive worse than a sinking. Bring it back here.
dialogue-celeste-have-chest: I have your booty from the Crimson Rose.
dialogue-celeste-remind-quest: Where did the Crimson Rose go down again?
dialogue-celeste-crimsonrose-reminder: Follow the map I gave you. The wreck is out there somewhere, find the chest or don't.
dialogue-celeste-crimsonrose-lostmap: You lost the map? Unbelievable. Here, I have another copy. Try not to lose this one.
dialogue-celeste-crimsonrose-received: You actually got my loot back? I wasn't expecting that I guess I'll give it to Morgan she'll find a use for it, what am I going to do with it now anyway.
dialogue-celeste-rumtrade-intro: If you've got rum to spare I'll gladly swap a decent drink for a treasure map
dialogue-celeste-offer-rum-whiterum: I'll trade a bottle of white rum for a treasure map.
dialogue-celeste-offer-rum-darkrum: I'll trade a bottle of dark rum for a treasure map.
dialogue-celeste-rumtrade-thanks: Aye, a fair trade. Here's a map, should keep you busy a while.
dialogue-celeste-ask-pirate-tales: I'd love to hear some of your stories. Rum?
dialogue-celeste-piratetales-mate: Did I ever tell you about my first mate, Robert the Red? He used to wear a little steering wheel on his belt.
dialogue-celeste-piratetales-wheel: He said it was driving his nuts. *Laughs loudly.*
dialogue-celeste-piratetales-booty: You think that's bad? The first time we met, he tried to grab my booty.
dialogue-celeste-piratetales-hooked: Neither was he. Who'd think a one-handed guy would do that? But what could I do? He had me hooked.
dialogue-celeste-piratetales-arrrated: I probably should. It gets a little Arrr-rated from here on.
dialogue-celeste-piratetales-plank: I want to walk the plank.
dialogue-celeste-piratetales-octopi: Watch out for octopi down there, they're the most well-armed creatures of the deep.
dialogue-celeste-ask-rusthunter: I hear you're looking to make the port safer.
dialogue-celeste-ask-rusthunter-active: I've got an update on the drifter problem.
dialogue-celeste-rusthunter-intro: I am. Those rust monsters attack every night. I do my best, but maybe if we thin their numbers it'll help, call it ten to start
dialogue-celeste-deliver-rot: I've got more rot for you.
dialogue-celeste-rusthunter-status: How many do I need to kill?
dialogue-celeste-rusthunter-statusreport: That's {player.quest-seafarer-celeste-rusthunter-kills-progress} of 10 rust monsters. Keep at it.
dialogue-celeste-rusthunter-reward: Quieter nights, finally. Here, I had this tucked away for a rainy day. Consider it earned.
dialogue-celeste-rusthunter-already-done: You've done your part against the rust. The port sleeps easier now.
dialogue-celeste-ask-bearhunter: Can you show me some hunting tricks?
dialogue-celeste-bearhunter-intro: No. Maybe. I don't teach beginners. But show me what you can do, bring me back 3 different bear pelts, head and all, and I'll show you some tricks.
dialogue-celeste-bearhunter-start: Good. Three different bears, head still on the pelt. I'll know if you skimp.
dialogue-celeste-deliver-pelt-polar: A polar bear pelt, head and all.
dialogue-celeste-deliver-pelt-brown: A brown bear pelt, head and all.
dialogue-celeste-deliver-pelt-black: A black bear pelt, head and all.
dialogue-celeste-deliver-pelt-panda: A panda bear pelt, head and all.
dialogue-celeste-deliver-pelt-sun: A sun bear pelt, head and all.
dialogue-celeste-bearhunter-status: How many pelts do you need?
dialogue-celeste-bearhunter-statusreport: Bring me pelts from three different kinds, head and all.
dialogue-celeste-bearhunter-pelt-thanks: A fine pelt. I'll find a wall for it.
dialogue-celeste-bearhunter-reward: These'll look good on my wall. Barely had anything to wear, at least now I can bare all.
dialogue-celeste-bearhunter-already-done: You've earned your stripes, hunter. The wall's full enough.
dialogue-drake-welcome: Hm. Another one looking for a ship, I'd wager. Well, you've come to the right place. I'm the only shipwright in Tortuga, and if you want anything bigger than a log raft, you'll need what I sell.
dialogue-drake-welcomeback: Back again. Need more supplies for that vessel of yours?
dialogue-drake-name: Drake. I build ships. Or rather, I sell what you need to build them yourself. Schematics, fittings, rope, planks, the works.
dialogue-drake-profession: I'm a shipwright. Any fool can lash logs together for a raft, but a real ship takes proper materials and know-how. Bring me wood, especially rare timber, and I'll trade you what you need.
dialogue-drake-ships: What ships can you help me build?
dialogue-drake-ships-info: You probably think lashing logs together is all it takes to build a boat. But trust me, you want it to last, you need seasoned wood, resin, and caulk.
dialogue-drake-goodbye: Bring wood next time. I always need more.
dialogue-drake-ask-tradeship: Can I help build a new trade ship?
dialogue-drake-tradeship-intro: Well voyager, if you want to help us get trade back up and running what we really need is a new sailboat. I can build a magnificent one if you can get the materials together. I've got some old tools kicking around somewhere, while you're getting the materials I'll dig them up.
dialogue-drake-tradeship-start: Right then. I need birch boards for the hull, rope for the rigging, and linen for the sails. Bring them to me as you find them, I'll keep a tally.
dialogue-drake-deliver-boards: I have birch boards for the hull.
dialogue-drake-deliver-rope: I brought rope for the rigging.
dialogue-drake-boards-thanks: Good timber. I'll add these to the pile.
dialogue-drake-boards-status: That's {global.quest-seafarer-drake-tradeship-boards-progress} of 298 birch boards. Keep them coming.
dialogue-drake-boards-complete: That's all the boards we need! The hull is taking shape. Here, found these old tools while clearing out the workshop.
dialogue-drake-rope-status: That's {global.quest-seafarer-drake-tradeship-rope-progress} of 27 coils of rope. Need more for the rigging.
dialogue-drake-rope-complete: Rigging is sorted! Dug up a saw and shears for your trouble.
dialogue-drake-linen-thanks: Good quality cloth. Should make fine sails.
dialogue-drake-linen-status: That's {global.quest-seafarer-drake-tradeship-linen-progress} of 21 linen squares. The sails need more.
dialogue-drake-linen-complete: The sails are done! Found a pickaxe and prospecting pick in the back of the shop, they're yours.
dialogue-drake-tradeship-statusreport: Hull needs {global.quest-seafarer-drake-tradeship-boards-progress}/298 boards. Rigging needs {global.quest-seafarer-drake-tradeship-rope-progress}/27 rope. Sails need {global.quest-seafarer-drake-tradeship-linen-progress}/21 linen. Bring what you can.
dialogue-drake-tradeship-complete: I'm surprised, didn't really think you'd get this done but you've made a huge difference. She's a beauty. Trade will be flowing again before you know it.
dialogue-drake-tradeship-already-done: The ship's built and ready to sail. You did good work, voyager.
dialogue-drake-blackbronze-unlock: Tortuga's on her feet again, and the forge has been humming. We have some new good for sale now.
dialogue-drake-ask-tricks: Can you teach me to build ships?
dialogue-drake-tricks-intro: Hmm, I guess I have the time now that we aren't struggling.
dialogue-drake-tricks-lesson1: I can teach you some tricks to better wood using seasoned wood, varnish, and oiled canvas sails.
dialogue-drake-tricks-lesson2: Treating the hull with resin or shellac is essential if you don't want more leaks than a cheesecloth.
dialogue-drake-tricks-lesson3: Lastly, oiling and waxing your sail will make it last longer and go faster.
dialogue-drake-tricks-varnish-task: Bring me 18 resin and 6 rendered fat and I'll teach you how to make marine varnish.
dialogue-drake-tricks-canvas-task: Then we'll need 20 linen and 5 oil to make the oiled canvas.
dialogue-drake-varnish-complete1: Great, I've got an old crusty pot we can use. We'll just cook it up until the resin melts. If you don't have fat, oil works just as well.
dialogue-drake-varnish-complete2: Just be sure to stir it until it's thick and melted, then you can apply it directly to wood or the hull of your ship.
dialogue-drake-varnish-complete3: Your ship will be a bit faster but mainly it'll be watertight and won't sink like a stone.
dialogue-drake-ask-canvas: Can you show me how to make the oiled canvas now?
dialogue-drake-canvas-complete1: Sure, it's pretty simple. You take the canvas, shove it in a barrel of oil in a ratio of 4 linen to 1 oil, and seal it overnight.
dialogue-drake-canvas-complete2: You'll get some nice waterproof canvas. If you rub wax onto it you can make it even stronger.
dialogue-drake-canvas-complete3: Here, take that oiled canvas and sew in 4 meters of rope. Bring me back that sail to show me you know what you're doing.
dialogue-drake-sail-review: Not bad. Your stitching could use work, and you'll want to reinforce it here and here to prevent tearing, but you've got the knack for it.
dialogue-drake-ask-seasoned: I've got the knack of ship building, now can you teach me how to build a decent boat?
dialogue-drake-seasoned-task1: Sure, bring me 160 planks of seasoned wood and I'll teach you to make an outrigger, a fast lightweight fishing boat.
dialogue-drake-seasoned-task2: Seasoning wood takes a long time, but it's well worth it if you want a boat to last. Stack up your lumber outside in the wind, cover it to keep the rain off, and in a month or two you'll have seasoned wood.
dialogue-drake-seasoned-complete: You really are a hard worker. Here, take this schematic for an outrigger. I'll sell you a new one if you need it.
dialogue-drake-deliver-seasoned: Here is the seasoned wood you asked for.
dialogue-reva-welcome: Interesting... You carry yourself like someone who's seen things beneath the waves. I deal in rare and precious things, gold, gems, artifacts. If you've found treasure, I'll give you a fair price. And if you want something truly special, well... browse my collection.
dialogue-reva-welcomeback: Ah, you return. I hope you've brought something shiny for me.
dialogue-reva-name: Call me Reva. I'm a collector of rare and beautiful things. Gold, silver, gems, I appreciate their worth better than most.
dialogue-reva-profession: I buy treasure, gold nuggets, silver, gems, anything precious you've found. In return, I sell rare items you won't find anywhere else. Unique pieces, decorative treasures, things with real character.
dialogue-reva-treasures: What kind of rare items do you have?
dialogue-reva-treasures-info: I acquire rare decorative pieces, ornaments, and curiosities from across the known world. Each piece is unique. My prices reflect their rarity, but then again, you can't put a price on something truly one of a kind.
dialogue-reva-goodbye: Until next time. Keep your eyes open for treasure, it's everywhere if you know where to look.
dialogue-jones-welcome: G'day mate! Welcome to the Last Resort Bar and Pizzeria. Pull up a stool, I'll get you sorted. Still can't quite believe this place is real, but the ale's wet and the fish is fresh, so who's complaining?
dialogue-jones-welcomeback: Oi, you're back! Didn't wipe out between here and there, I hope.
dialogue-jones-name: Name's Jones, mate. Used to chase the perfect barrel back home, then — crikey, I dunno — one minute I'm paddling out at sunrise, next minute I'm in this rust-cursed world pourin' drinks for strangers. Reckon I must've caught the wrong wave.
dialogue-jones-profession: I run the Last Resort. Bar, pizzeria, whatever you need. Well — the 'pizzeria' part's a bit aspirational, truth be told. Ovens round here are a bloody shocker, mate. Workin' on it. For now, it's tavern grub and whatever drinks I can rustle up.
dialogue-jones-world: Is any of this actually real, mate?
dialogue-jones-world-info: Dunno, grommet. One day I'm watchin' the sun come up over Bondi, next I'm hearin' about temporal storms and rust and towers. Ain't no rust on a surfboard where I'm from, I'll tell ya that. Sometimes I reckon I'm still out there floatin', and this whole show's just a dream I'm havin' while the water carries me somewhere. Either way — the ale's good. That's what matters.
dialogue-jones-goodbye: Hooroo, mate. Catch a wave for me, yeah?
dialogue-dawnmarie-welcome: Oh... a visitor. I didn't think anyone new came here anymore. I suppose you're looking to trade? I don't have much, but what I have is honest work.
dialogue-dawnmarie-welcomeback: You came back. I wasn't sure you would.
dialogue-dawnmarie-name: Dawn Marie. I used to paint, portraits, landscapes, anything beautiful. Now I grow what I can and try not to think about what I've lost.
dialogue-dawnmarie-profession: I tend the plantation at the edge of town. Flowers, sugar cane, a few olive trees. I sell seeds and cuttings, and I'll buy any paintings you find. They remind me of better days.
dialogue-dawnmarie-goodbye: Take care out there. The world is harder than it used to be.
dialogue-dawnmarie-ask-orchard: Need any help with the orchard?
dialogue-dawnmarie-orchard-intro: There were so many beautiful fruit trees here at one time. I used to spend hours painting under them. Bring me fruit tree cuttings and I can start replanting.
dialogue-dawnmarie-orchard-start: Anything you can find — apples, cherries, peaches, pears, oranges, mangoes, breadfruit, lychee, pomegranate, coconut. Any six different kinds will be enough to get us started.
dialogue-dawnmarie-deliver-orchard-pinka: I have a pink apple cutting.
dialogue-dawnmarie-deliver-orchard-redap: I have a red apple cutting.
dialogue-dawnmarie-deliver-orchard-yello: I have a yellow apple cutting.
dialogue-dawnmarie-orchard-delivery-than: Another tree for the orchard! Thank you, I'll plant it right away.
dialogue-dawnmarie-orchard-statusreport: Bring me cuttings from any six different fruit trees. Anything helps.
dialogue-dawnmarie-orchard-complete: The orchard is alive again. I can hardly believe it. Paint it someday, voyager, it deserves to be remembered.
dialogue-dawnmarie-ask-plantation: Need any help with the plantation?
dialogue-dawnmarie-plantation-intro: More food variety would help everyone out. Bring me 10 seeds of any crop and I can start growing them.
dialogue-dawnmarie-plantation-start: Rice, soybeans, amaranth, cassava, peanuts, pineapple, sunflower, rye, parsnip, turnip, spelt, onion, flax, carrot, cabbage, pumpkin, licorice — any six different kinds, ten seeds each.
dialogue-dawnmarie-plantation-delivery-t: Good, good. I'll get these in the ground before the season changes.
dialogue-dawnmarie-plantation-statusrepo: Bring me ten seeds of any six different crops. We need variety to feed everyone.
dialogue-dawnmarie-plantation-complete: The fields will feed us again. Proper food, not just dried fish and hardtack.
quest-dawnmarie-orchard-desc: Dawn Marie wants to replant the orchard. Bring her cuttings from six different fruit trees.
quest-dawnmarie-plantation-desc: Dawn Marie needs seeds to rebuild the plantation. Bring her ten seeds each of six different crops.
quest-potatoking-lastpotato-desc: The Potato King won't sign Morgan's contract until someone finds him a potato seed. He's marked an abandoned farm on the map — go dig around in the ruins.
dialogue-potatoking-welcome: Is that you old friend? Wait no, I thought you were my brother of the rails.
dialogue-potatoking-welcomeback: You again? Unless you've got something important, leave me to my work. I'm trying to crack the problem of ships the sail through the air.
dialogue-potatoking-name: They call me the Potato King. I was streamer in the world. Now I get focus on my dream of automated mining and mineral processing. But god I'd kill for a potato.
dialogue-potatoking-show-letter: I have a letter from Morgan.
dialogue-potatoking-letter-response: A letter from Morgan, eh? Let me see that... 'Dear old friend, please resume mining operations, the port needs you, blah blah blah.' She always was an optimist.
dialogue-potatoking-quest-start: Fine. I'll make you a deal. There's one seed potato left, ONE!, in an abandoned farm out in the ruins. The temporal storms scattered everything. Find me that seed and I'll sign whatever contract Morgan wants. Here's a map.
dialogue-potatoking-have-seed: I found the last potato seed!
dialogue-potatoking-quest-reminder: The map I gave you points to an abandoned farm. Find that potato seed! Without it, no deal.
dialogue-potatoking-lostmap: You lost the map?! Bah. Here, another copy. Try to keep this one in one piece.
dialogue-potatoking-seed-received: Is that... is that really it? A seed potato! Oh glorious day! I'll plant it, propagate it, the kingdom will eat again!
dialogue-potatoking-give-contract: A deal is a deal. Here's the signed contract for Morgan. Tell her the mine will be operational again. And tell her I expect a steady supply of potatoes once my crop's grown!
dialogue-potatoking-goodbye: Bah. Don't come back without potatoes.
dialog-morgan-sugar-status: Thank you kindly, that's {entity.sugar} of 10 loads of sugar that we need.
itemdesc-boat-outrigger: A fast, lightweight twin-hulled fishing boat. Two seats and a single sail.
item-handbooktext-boat-outrigger-*:
Outrigger canoes are constructed in place on land using building rollers, then rolled into the water. They cannot be picked up. They can be deconstructed by holding down rightmouse with a saw.

Construction
Craft the outrigger schematic with rope and firewood to obtain building rollers. Place the rollers on water-adjacent ground to begin: an in-world schematic will appear. You can change the direction the schematic faces using the toolmodeselect key. With block interaction help on (toggle with blockinteractionhelp), looking at the in-process boat will show you the next required ingredients. You will need seasoned or varnished planks, support beams of the same wood type, firewood, rope, and linen for the sail.

Movement
Once launched, rightmouse the helmsman seat at the stern to take the tiller — only that seat controls the boat. With sails furled, normal navigation keys move the boat slowly. rightmouse the cleat on the side of the mast to unfurl the sail and pick up speed. The outrigger does not respond to wind direction, only your movement keys.

Equipping
Most accessories are added via ctrl + rightmouse. The outrigger seats two passengers in addition to the helmsman. Cargo spots accept chests, baskets, storage vessels, lanterns, grow pots, and barrels. The fore cargo position accepts a rope tie post for tethering animals. Forward and rear lantern mounts provide light. The sail mount accepts a sail recolor accessory.
itemdesc-boat-outrigger-rollers: Place on water-adjacent ground to begin building an outrigger.
item-handbooktext-boat-outrigger-rollers: Roller logs are placed on water-adjacent ground to begin construction of an outrigger canoe. Pay attention to the placement preview, and make sure the blue highlight cubes are in water. Use the toolmodeselect key to change the orientation of the preview blocks.
handbook-seafarer-outrigger-craftinfo: The outrigger schematic is a quest reward from Drake (deliver 160 seasoned planks). Drake also resells the schematic. Craft the schematic with rope and firewood to get rollers, then place the rollers near water to begin construction.
seafarer:boatrepair-fullhp: This boat is already in perfect condition.
seafarer:boat-no-sail-slot: This boat can't use a sail.
